retrieved_docs.title: Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing -- Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: A random error of up to 5% is acce­ptable. If encountere­d, laboratories should promptly redo quality control testing. When the error is resolve­d, regular testing can resume­. However, persiste­nt errors require imme­diate action to identify the root cause­, and patient results should not be re­ported until resolved, which may involve­ testing new quality control strains, evaluating additional mate­rials, or consulting with commercial antimicrobial susceptibility te­sting equipment manufacturers. Laboratories may ne­ed to switch to alternative te­sting methods until the issue is fixe­d. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Iron-Binding Capacity -- Testing Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: UIBC-based methods generally exhibit a significant negative bias compared to TIBC-based methods. [24] The chemical methods for TIBC require a relatively large sample volume. The TIBC assays are sensitive to contamination of laboratory consumables with Fe and show a large variation; reference intervals differ by as much as 35% among commercial methods. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Toxicology Screening -- Diagnostic Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Toxicology screens have changed markedly over the years. Methods such as gas chromatography and radioimmunoassays have given way in everyday use to enzyme-linked sorbent immunoassay (ELISA) and cloned enzyme donor immunoassay (CEDIA). This migration is largely due to speed and ease of use. However, these new generation immunoassays carry with them limitations in the form of reduced sensitivity and specificity. The calibration of these tests can detect specific substances rather than an entire class of drugs and also suffer from cross-reactivity to structurally similar compounds. Comprehensive drug screens utilizing other methods tend to be prohibitive in terms of expense and typically can take weeks to result, making them impractical for clinical use. [4] [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1610
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic agents for bile duct patency testing and gastric secretion test diagnostic agents are both classified as “other diagnostic agents in ATC.” These agents are used for specialized diagnostic procedures: the former assess the openness of the bile ducts, while the latter evaluate gastric secretory function. By being grouped under the broader ATC category of other diagnostic agents, they share a common regulatory and therapeutic classification despite serving distinct clinical testing purposes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1965
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroid dermatological preparations, plain encompass a range of topical corticosteroid strengths that are classified by potency groups. Within this category, “Corticosteroids, weak (group I)” represent the low‑potency end of the spectrum, while “Corticosteroids, very potent (group IV)” represent the high‑potency end. This hierarchical classification allows clinicians to select an appropriate plain corticosteroid preparation based on the severity of the skin condition, aiming to achieve therapeutic effectiveness while minimizing the risk of adverse effects associated with higher‑potency steroids.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 775
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic agents for bile duct patency testing, gastric secretion test diagnostic agents, indokyanová zeleň, and urea C‑13 are all classified as members of the “other diagnostic agents in ATC” category. This ATC subgroup groups together a diverse set of substances used for specialized diagnostic purposes, ranging from agents that assess bile duct openness and evaluate gastric secretory function to an herbal preparation (indokyanová zeleň) and an isotopically labeled compound (urea C‑13). Recognizing these agents as part of a common ATC class aids in their systematic identification, regulatory oversight, and appropriate clinical use in diagnostic testing.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1621
retrieved_docs.content: Glucose 100 mg/mL injection is formulated with dextrose as its pharmacologically active component; anhydrous dextrose constitutes the active moiety of the preparation, while dextrose monohydrate serves as the active ingredient. Both forms of dextrose provide the therapeutic glucose delivered by the injection, ensuring consistent dosing and efficacy in clinical use.
